coredata.dc:title: Battery state-of-charge estimation using fractional extended Kalman filter with Mittag-Leffler memory
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.
coredata.prism:issueName: New trends of numerical and analytical methods for engineering problems
coredata.dc:creator: Hidalgo-Reyes, J.I.
Gómez-Aguilar, J.F.
Alvarado-Martínez, V.M.
López-López, M.G.
Escobar-Jiménez, R.F.
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a Fractional-Order Extended Kalman Filter (FOEKF) to estimate the State-of-Charge (SOC) of a LiFePO4 battery is proposed. Today there is no sensor to measure the state of charge, due to this, it is necessary to use various methods to calculate an estimated value of this parameter. For designing the FOEKF a battery model based on an equivalent electric circuit and the fractional derivative of Atangana-Baleanu type were used. Different numerical simulations using Matlab® were carried out in order to compare it with experimental results from a laboratory prototype and show the performance and accuracy of the FOEKF for estimating the SOC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LiFePO4 battery
Extended Kalman filter
Fractional calculus
State-of-charge

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the constructive remarks and suggestions of the anonymous referees that helped to improve the paper. Jorge Ivan Hidalgo Reyes acknowledges the support provided by CONACyT through the assignment doctoral fellowship. José Francisco Gómez Aguilar and Victor Manuel Alvarado Martínez acknowledges the support provided by CONACyT: cátedras CONACyT para jóvenes investigadores 2014. José Francisco Gómez Aguilar, Victor Manuel Alvarado Martínez, Guadalupe López López and Ricardo Fabricio Escobar Jiménez acknowledges the support provided by SNI-CONACyT.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.

head.ce:title: Battery state-of-charge estimation using fractional extended Kalman filter with Mittag-Leffler memory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tecnológico Nacional de México/CENIDET, Interior Internado Palmira S/N, Col. Palmira, C.P. 62490 Cuernavaca, Morelos, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tecnológico Nacional de México/CENIDET, Interior Internado Palmira S/N, Col. Palmira, C.P. 62490, Cuernavaca, Morelos, México

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CONACyT-Tecnológico Nacional de México/CENIDET, Interior Internado Palmira S/N, Col. Palmira, C.P. 62490 Cuernavaca, Morelos, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CONACyT-Tecnológico Nacional de México/CENIDET, Interior Internado Palmira S/N, Col. Palmira, C.P. 62490, Cuernavaca, Morelos, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a Fractional-Order Extended Kalman Filter (FOEKF) to estimate the State-of-Charge (SOC) of a LiFePO4 battery is proposed. Today there is no sensor to measure the state of charge, due to this, it is necessary to use various methods to calculate an estimated value of this parameter. For designing the FOEKF a battery model based on an equivalent electric circuit and the fractional derivative of Atangana-Baleanu type were used. Different numerical simulations using Matlab® were carried out in order to compare it with experimental results from a laboratory prototype and show the performance and accuracy of the FOEKF for estimating the SOC.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy Storage Systems (ESS) aim to store energy to be used later for a specific purpose. Nowadays, there are different ESS available which are classified as follows [1]: mechanical systems, chemical systems, electrochemical systems, electrical systems, and thermal systems, this work focus on the Lithium Iron Phosphate (LiFePO4) batteries due to their characteristics like high energy density, modularity, lightweight, fast response, controllability, geographical independence, higher cycle life, and performance [2,3].

The main objective of the Battery Management System (BMS) is to increase the cycle-life, to optimize the power delivery and the battery safety [4]. The BMS monitors and measures the cell voltages, temperature and current in order to prevent the overcharge and overdischarge, detect unsafe operating conditions and increase the cycle life. Besides, the BMS should consider the SOC, then this information enables the cell balance and maximizing the performance of the battery.

In the literature different techniques for estimating the battery’s SOC have been reported. Summarizing, the SOC is estimated by a physical model, an empirical model, or even an abstract model [5]. In [6], the authors considered a dynamic equivalent circuit model (2-RC branch model) to describe the device electrical features. The results obtained by several simulations shown that this model represents the battery output-voltage accurately. For this reason, in this work we consider the equivalent electric circuit model described by Liao in [6].

Regarding the SOC estimation task, in the literature, different studies applying the EKF approach have been reported. In [7], an EEC for LFP battery and combined with an EKF and the traditional Coulomb counting was used to compute the SOC. This system was simulated using the SimScape and Simulink programming tools and the resulting predictions were compared with experimental data (of a current profile from modified New European Drive Cycle (NEDC). The SOC estimation was achieved within 4 % of the experimental SOC. Authors in [8] developed also an EKF for estimating the SCO. In this work, the components of the EEC were considered to be dependent on the SOC. Moreover, an energy-balance circuit is included. With the aim to assess the method, several simulations were performed and the results compared with the SOC resulting from the Coulomb counting method. Furthermore, experimental data from a LiFePO4 battery was used to quantify the performance of the EKF. The document concludes that the EKF estimates the SOC and the battery voltage with mean errors less than 1.1% and 44 mV, respectively. Likewise, using the same equivalent electrical circuit, in [9], the authors proposed an EKF with the same objective, but the Federal Urban Driving Schedule (FUDS) drive cycle was employed to analyze its performance. From the results it was observed that the error between the experimental and simulated SOC was approximately 0.01, with a filter response of the order of milliseconds. Other works reported in the literature used the EKF for estimating the parameters of the two-branch EEC modeling Ion batteries [10,11]. In [12], a different EEC model was used to design the EKF for SOC estimation, the authors chose the Thevenin electric circuit and the Coulomb counting method to formulate an EKF. A Dynamic Stress Test (DST) was selected to check the functioning of the nonlinear filter. EKF accuracy to estimate the SOC was evaluated by the absolute error, which not exceed 2 %.

The reliability of the EKF to estimate the batteries SOC was widely demonstrated in the literature. An effective way to improve this estimation is to introduce Fractional Calculus (FC). FC is a generalization of the classical derivatives and integrals. The main advantage of fractional operators is their non-local characteristic that allows describing systems with hereditary and memory properties [13–19]. Some works related to the development of Fractional-Order Kalman filters are now described. In [20], the authors proposed a FOKF to estimate linear and nonlinear discrete state-space systems. The main improvements resulted in high accuracy and robustness to noise. In [21], the authors proposed a fractional EKF to synchronize and encrypt chaotic systems for secure communication applications. In [22], Sierociuk improved a FOKF using the infinite-dimensional representation of a linear discrete-time fractional-order state-space model. As a result, better approximation and smoother estimation of the parameters were achieved. The EKF has also been used to estimate state variables for ultracapacitors [23,24] or batteries [25,26] models. In all the cases, one of the major issues is to decide which fractional derivative should be used. Different definitions of fractional derivatives can be found in the literature, e.g., the Riemann-Liouville-Caputo with a singular kernel, Caputo-Fabrizio with a non-singular-local kernel and Atangana-Baleanu with a Mittag-Leffler type kernel. This last derivative is free from many limitations and very effective to describe real world issues [27,28].

The main purpose of this paper is to generalize an EKF for SOC estimation of a LiFePO4 battery by introducing the Atangana-Baleanu fractional derivative in the EKF design. The modified fractional EKF is applied to estimate the SOC, in such a way that experimental measurements from a Li-Ion battery match with an equivalent circuit model with non-constant parameters dependent on the SOC. The manuscript is composed in the following way: in Section 2 are presented the battery model using an equivalent electrical circuit and the laboratory test bench for parameter identification. Sections 3 and 4, show the fractional-order EKF algorithm design, and the numerical simulations for evaluating the nonlinear observer performance and accuracy, respectively. Finally, in Section 5, some concluding remarks are presented.
To compute the SOC, a mathematical model is necessary. In this work, we consider for the battery a model using an EEC as proposed in [6].

Fig. 1 shows the 2-RC branch model. This model represents the polarization effect and the short-time and long-time constants,
R
i
models the internal resistance and the instantaneous voltage drop when a load is connected to the battery. The open-circuit voltage (OCV) of the battery is represented as an ideal dependent-voltage source. Since the battery presents nonlinear characteristics, all the elements in the EEC usually are a function of: temperature, age, current or number of cycle, but, the main dependence is associated to the SOC and therefore all the elements are defined in terms of this variable.

By using the Kirchhoff’s Voltage Law (LVK) in the EEC, the battery model is as follows

The model parameters were estimated using the Nonlinear Least-Squares (NLS) curve fitting obtained from the experimental data get at room temperature from a laboratory test-bench with the methodology proposed in [29]. We considered the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm for solving the NLS, with the Sum of Squared Errors (SSE) as the objective function.

Fig. 2 a shows the laboratory prototype connection for charging process; meanwhile, Fig. 2b shows the connection for discharging process. The components of the laboratory prototype are the following:
E1 DC power supply (Agilent) E2 DC electronic load (BK Precision)
E3 Signal conditionign module (Red Lion) E4 Current sensor (ACS712)
E5 Arduino nano E6 Arduino mega 2650
E7 LiFePO4 battery

The input signal for the experiments was a 9A pulse current signal with 50 % of duty cycle and 60 min of the period, which allows discharging the battery 10 % of SOC per cycle. The sampling time was set at 0.5 S/s.

The coefficients values of Eqs. (5)–(10) are shown in Table 1 , where
e
min
represents the minimum error in the setting,
e
max
represents the maximum error in the setting,
S
e
represents the standard deviation of the relative error, and FIT is defined in Eq. (38).
Fig. 3 shows the experimental OCV and the fitted curve using the previous coefficients in the OCV function. Table 2 shows the performance index.

The EKF is a filter for systems with nonlinear dynamics which linearizes by a first-order Taylor series at every instant of time, i.e., it transforms the nonlinear system in a LTV system. In this section, we present the EKF design for the battery SOC estimation considering the Atangana-Baleanu fractional-order derivative. The fractional derivative of Atangana-Baleanu-Caputo type (ABC) with order
( α > 0 )
is defined as follows [30]
(11)
ABC
a

is the Atangana-Baleanu kernel (AB) defined by

,
( α > 0 ) ,
and
B ( α )
is a normalization function such that
B ( 0 ) = B ( 1 ) = 1
.

To develop the FOEKF that estimates the battery SOC, consider the next discrete version of the CEE model showed in Fig. 1. After performing the procedure described in [33], we have

In this section, numerical simulations results that were computed in Matlab® are shown. Two cases were considered, on the one hand, a charge/discharge constant-current test. On the other hand, a charge/discharge pulse-current test at different current values (9A, 11A, and 13A).

The optimal order of the fractional derivative
α
was obtained by the Firefly Algorithm (FA), proposed in [34]. This algorithm is a metaheuristic method inspired on the flashing light of fireflies tha is used in optimization problems. This particular characteristic is formulated as an objective function. It is a bioluminescence process produced by fireflies whose behavior can be described by the following rules,
•
One firefly is attracted to other fireflies regardless of their sex.
•
Attractiveness is proportional to their brightness, such that the less bright firefly moves towards the brighter one. If there is no brighter firefly than this, the less bright firefly moves randomly.
•
The landscape of the objective function affects or determines the firefly’s brightness.
Algorithm 1
Firefly algorithm [34].
1: Objective function
f ( x ) , x =
(
x
1
, … ,
x
d
)
T
is defined
2: Initial population of fireflies
x
i
( i = 1 , 2 , … , n )
is generated
3:
f (
x
i
)
determines the light intensity
I
i
at
x
i
4: The light absorption coefficient is defined as
γ
5: while t
<
MaxGeneration
6: for i = 1:n all n fireflies
7:
for j = 1:n all n fireflies (inner loop)
8:
if
I
i
<
I
j
9:
Move firefly i towards j;
10:
end if
11:
Vary attractiveness with distance r via
exp ( - γ r )
12: Evaluate new solutions and update light intensity
13: Rank the fireflies and find the current global best
g
∗

Past algorithm shows the pseudo-code of the FA. For general purposes, the attractiveness
β
of a firefly is determined by its brightness and associated with the encoded objective. Light intensity,
I ( r )
, is absorbed in the media and decreasing with the distance from its source and is defined as:
(34)
I ( r ) =
I
s
r
2
,

where
I
s
is the intensity at the source and r the variable distance.

Firefly’s attractiveness is defined as
(35)
β =
β
0
e
- γ
r
2
,

where
β
0
is the attractiveness when
r = 0
. In addition, the variation of the attractiveness is defined by
γ
and specifies the convergence rate of the FA.

The Cartesian distance
(36)
r
ij
= ‖
x
i
-
x
j
‖ =
∑
k = 1
d
(
x
i , k
-
x
j , k
)
2
,
represents the distance between two any fireflies i and j at
x
i
, and
x
j
respectively. Moreover,
x
i , k
is the k-th component of the spatial coordinate
x
i
of i-th firefly.

The movement of a firefly i which is attracted by another brighter firefly j is determined by the relation
(37)
x
i
=
x
i
+
β
0
e
- γ
r
ij
2
(
x
j
-
x
i
) + ϖ
∊
i
,
where
ϖ
is the randomization parameter and
∊
i
is a vector of random numbers from a Gaussian distribution.

The fractional-order value (
α
) was optimized by the FA under the Sum of Squared Errors and subject to the constraint
α ∈ ( 0 , 1 )
. In addition, in order to evaluate the performance the FIT function was used. This function computes the percentage of the output variation and is defined as
(38)
FIT =
1 -
‖ y ( t ) -
ν
̂
( t ) ‖
‖ y ( t ) -
y ( t )
¯
) ‖
· 100 %

Case 1 - Constant current charging. - For this case, we consider that the battery is completely discharged and therefore the SOC is at 0%. A constant current was provide to the battery until it’s voltage value is 14.5 V. Numerical simulation results along with the experimental data are shown in Fig. 4 a-d considering 9A current and different
α
values. The parameters and inital conditions used in the numerical simulations, as well as the index performances are presented in Tables 5 and 6
.

Case 2 - Constant current discharging. - Now, we consider that the battery is completely charged and therefore the SOC is at 100%. A constant current (11A) was provide to the battery to bring it from 14.5 V to 11 V (Discharging Cut-Off Voltage). The simulation results versus the experimental data are shown in Fig. 5 a–d. The parameters considered in the numerical simulations are the same as before, but the initial conditions are shown in Table 7 . Finally, the index performances are presented in Table 8 .

Case 3 - Pulse current charging. – In this case, we consider that the battery is completely discharged and therefore the SOC is at 0%. A pulse-current (9A) with a duty cycle of 50% and a period of 3600 s was provide to the battery until it’s voltage value is 14.5 V. Numerical simulation results along with the experimental data are shown in Fig. 6 b–d. The parameters considered in the numerical simulations are the same as case 1 and the initial conditions are presented in Tables 9 and 10
.
The index performances for this test are shown in Table 10.

Case 4 - Pulse current discharging. - Now, we consider that the battery is completely charged and therefore the SOC is at 100%. A pulse-current (13A) with a period of 2494 s and duty cycle of 50% was provide to the battery to bring it from 14.5 V to 11 V. The simulation results versus the experimental data are shown in Fig. 7 a–d. The parameters used in the numerical simulations are the same as in case 2, and the index performances are presented in Tables 11 and 12
.

A FOKF algorithm with Mittag-Leffler memory for the SOC estimation of LiFePO4 battery was proposed in this paper. An EEC model was used to describe the nonlinear dynamics of the battery. Algorithm proposed is an extension of the Fractional Kalman Filter algorithm involving Atangana-Baleanu derivatives. Experimental results from a laboratory test-bench were employed in order to present the advantages of using an EKF to SOC estimation. Results showed high precision for the estimation of the EEC components and due to the Mittag–Leffler memory the observer is robust to noise. Moreover, one advantage of using fractional derivatives is the order of the derivative
α
, since it characterizes the dynamical system’s memory and it’s considered as a free parameter.

The estimation of time-varying parameters and the use for adaptive control processes, battery reuse applications and battery online state estimation or prediction will be presented in further research.
The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest regarding the publication of this paper.
All authors contributed equally and significantly in writing this article. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate the constructive remarks and suggestions of the anonymous referees that helped to improve the paper. Jorge Ivan Hidalgo Reyes acknowledges the support provided by CONACyT through the assignment doctoral fellowship. José Francisco Gómez Aguilar and Victor Manuel Alvarado Martínez acknowledges the support provided by CONACyT: cátedras CONACyT para jóvenes investigadores 2014. José Francisco Gómez Aguilar, Victor Manuel Alvarado Martínez, Guadalupe López López and Ricardo Fabricio Escobar Jiménez acknowledges the support provided by SNI-CONACyT.
